red_under_diagnosis.failure_signature: test_f1_happy_path_oauth_login_establishes_session: flow cannot compose — CT1 (C2->C1) declares kind:async_event requiring a message broker to deliver the identity-write event, but no broker is wired; the OAuth callback has no synchronous acknowledgement to establish the session on.

flaky_check.evidence: Failure asserts a structural composition impossibility: CT1.kind=async_event requires a broker that cannot exist under INV6 (single-server synchronous, no background job queues — A13/ADR-0001). Three identical signatures, zero variance across attempts. Deterministic.

reflection_pass.finding: CT1 declares kind:async_event and shape 'publishes the identity-write event onto a queue and does not block on persistence.' This directly contradicts INV6 + A13 (single-server deployment with a synchronous request cycle; no background job queues) and ADR-0001 (no structure implying distributed components). The frozen oracle itself resolves CT1 as a synchronous shared_data seam (test_CT1.py calls save_identity directly; conftest patches identity_record_store with no broker fixture; test_F1.py patches save_identity directly). Wiring a broker to satisfy CT1.kind=async_event would both violate INV6 and break the frozen oracle. CT1.kind=async_event is the defect — unbuildable as frozen.

routable_diagnosis.change_request: Change CT1.kind from 'async_event' to 'shared_data'. Update CT1.shape to describe a synchronous in-process call: OAuth callback calls save_identity(provider, provider_id, profile_ref) and blocks on the acknowledgement before establishing the session; get_identity reads on session resolution. Honors INV6 (synchronous, no async/queue/broker). Rationale: INV6 + A13 prohibit background job queues; ADR-0001 forbids distributed structure; the frozen oracle already materializes CT1 as a synchronous shared_data seam; the implementation already calls save_identity synchronously. No other CT1 field requires change.
